section: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility
question: Your restaurant holds a PLB (Business Liquor by the Drink) license but not a Local Option Permit. A guest asks for a cocktail at 1 p.m. on Sunday. What is correct?
correct: B. Do not serve liquor by the drink on Sunday without a Local Option Permit
wrong: A. Serve it; liquor by the drink is always allowed
C. Serve only if they also order food
D. Serve a double instead
feedback: A PLB allows liquor Monday-Saturday. Sunday liquor by the drink requires a Local Option Permit (LOP), issued only where the county approved Sunday liquor sales.

section: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility
question: You are working at a restaurant. A customer asks if they can purchase a type of alcohol your location does not normally sell. You are unsure if your business is licensed for it. What should you do?
correct: B. Check with a manager before serving
wrong: A. Sell the alcohol if the customer insists
C. Guess based on past experience
D. Tell the customer it is always allowed
feedback: You must confirm what your establishment is licensed to sell. When unsure, always verify before serving. Guessing or assuming can lead to illegal service and potential penalties.

section: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility
question: A young-looking customer orders a drink. They say they forgot their ID but insist they are 21. Their friend offers to confirm their age. What should you do?
correct: B. Refuse service without valid ID
wrong: A. Serve the drink if the friend confirms
C. Serve one drink as a test
D. Ask them to come back later and serve now
feedback: You must verify age with valid identification. Verbal confirmation from others is not acceptable. Serving without ID risks an underage sale and legal consequences.

section: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility
question: You notice a customer becoming loud, unsteady, and slow to respond. They order another drink and mention they will drive home soon. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse service and suggest safe transportation
wrong: A. Serve the drink and trust their judgment
B. Delay service but serve eventually
D. Ignore the behavior and continue service
feedback: You must stop service when a customer shows signs of intoxication. Offering safe alternatives helps prevent impaired driving and supports legal compliance.

section: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility
question: You notice a customer with a visible firearm in an establishment that has posted restrictions against weapons. The customer is calm and not causing a disturbance. What should you do?
correct: C. Notify a manager and follow policy
wrong: A. Approach the customer and demand they leave
B. Ignore the situation completely
D. Ask other customers what to do
feedback: Your role is to observe and report. Management or law enforcement should handle enforcement. Avoid direct confrontation and follow workplace procedures to maintain safety and compliance.

section: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility
question: You serve a customer who shows clear signs of intoxication. Later, they are involved in an accident after leaving the business. What type of consequences could result?
correct: C. Criminal, civil, and administrative consequences
wrong: A. Only internal discipline
B. Only a warning from management
D. No consequences if the customer chose to drive
feedback: One incident can lead to multiple types of liability. Serving an intoxicated person increases risk of harm and may result in legal, financial, and licensing consequences.

section: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility
question: A customer who looks under 21 attempts to order alcohol but does not have an ID. They insist they are old enough. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse service without valid ID
wrong: A. Serve them if they seem confident
B. Ask another employee for their opinion and serve
D. Serve one drink and monitor
feedback: You must verify age with valid identification. Serving without ID risks an underage sale and legal consequences.

section: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility
question: A customer presents an ID that looks damaged and does not clearly match their appearance. They insist it is valid. What is the best action?
correct: B. Refuse service due to questionable ID
wrong: A. Accept the ID and serve
C. Ignore the damage and continue
D. Ask another customer for confirmation
feedback: You must refuse service if identification appears altered, damaged, or does not match the person presenting it.

section: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility
question: You notice a customer slurring their speech and struggling to stay balanced. They order another drink. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse service due to intoxication
wrong: A. Serve them quickly
B. Ignore the behavior
D. Ask them if they feel okay and serve
feedback: You must stop service when a customer shows signs of intoxication. Continuing to serve increases legal and safety risks.

section: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC
question: Two guests each had three drinks in an hour. One appears far more impaired than the other. What is the best explanation for a server to keep in mind?
correct: B. Factors like body weight, food, drinking rate, tolerance, and medications change how alcohol affects each person, so judge impairment by behavior
wrong: A. The calmer guest is safe to keep serving
C. They must have miscounted their drinks
D. BAC is the same for everyone after three drinks
feedback: BAC and visible impairment vary with body weight, biological sex, food, drinking rate, tolerance, and any drugs taken. Always judge impairment by observed behavior, not just the number of drinks.

section: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC
question: A guest orders several drinks quickly. Within a short time, you notice slower speech and unsteady movement. The guest insists they feel fine and asks for another drink. Based on alcohol absorption and effects, what is the safest action?
correct: D. Refuse further alcohol service
wrong: A. Serve one more drink slowly
B. Ignore behavior and continue service
C. Offer food and continue alcohol service
feedback: Refusing service is the safest choice when clear signs of impairment appear. Alcohol is already affecting the guest, and continued service increases risk. Servers must act based on observed behavior, not guest statements.

section: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC
question: A guest orders two strong mixed drinks in a short time. You notice faster speech and reduced coordination. The guest asks for another drink right away. Based on BAC and standard drink understanding, what is the safest action?
correct: B. Offer water and slow service
wrong: A. Serve another drink with extra ice
C. Ignore signs and continue service
D. Suggest switching to beer and continue
feedback: Slowing service and offering water helps reduce risk. Strong drinks increase BAC quickly, especially in a short time. Recognizing early signs and adjusting service supports responsible alcohol service and aligns with your role.

section: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC
question: A guest has been drinking slowly but mentions they have not eaten all day. After two drinks, you notice quicker mood changes and reduced coordination. The guest asks for another drink. What is the safest action?
correct: C. Slow or stop alcohol service based on signs
wrong: A. Serve another drink since intake is low
B. Offer food and continue normal service
D. Ignore food factor and continue service
feedback: Drinking on an empty stomach increases BAC more quickly. Even with fewer drinks, impairment can occur sooner. Observing changes and adjusting service helps prevent over-service and supports responsible alcohol service.

section: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC
question: A guest drinks several beverages and then orders coffee. They say they are fine to continue drinking because the coffee “sobers them up.” What is the safest response?
correct: D. Stop alcohol service based on impairment
wrong: A. Serve another drink since they had coffee
B. Allow time and continue normal service
C. Offer food and continue alcohol service
feedback: Coffee does not reduce BAC. The guest may feel more alert but is still impaired. Stopping alcohol service is the safest choice when signs of intoxication are present.

section: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC
question: A guest has one drink but shows strong drowsiness and poor coordination. They mention taking medication earlier. They ask for another drink. What is the safest action?
correct: D. Stop alcohol service based on impairment
wrong: A. Serve another drink since intake is low
B. Offer water and continue alcohol service
C. Ignore medication and continue service
feedback: Drug interactions can increase alcohol effects quickly. Even one drink may cause strong impairment. Stopping alcohol service is the safest choice when signs appear.

section: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC
question: A guest orders strong mixed drinks back to back. They appear alert but show poor judgment and risk-taking behavior. What is the best response?
correct: B. Offer water and monitor behavior
wrong: A. Continue service since they seem alert
C. Serve a weaker drink instead
D. Ignore behavior and continue service
feedback: Stimulant effects can mask impairment. Offering water and slowing service helps reduce risk while you continue to observe behavior.

section: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC
question: A guest says they “feel fine” after several drinks. You observe slurred speech and unsteady walking. What should guide your decision?
correct: C. Observed behavior
wrong: A. Guest’s statement
B. Number of drinks only
D. Time spent drinking
feedback: Observed behavior is the most reliable indicator of impairment. Always base decisions on what you see, not what the guest says.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: Today is July 6, 2026. A customer's ID shows a date of birth of September 2, 2005. Can you sell them alcohol?
correct: B. No -- they turn 21 on September 2, 2026, which is after today, so they are still under 21
wrong: A. Yes, 2005 means they are 21
C. Yes, if they also show a second ID
D. Only after 9 p.m.
feedback: The required date of birth today is July 6, 2005. A birth date of September 2, 2005 is after that, so the customer is still under 21 until September 2, 2026. Refuse the sale.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer hands you a license. The photo hair and face look different, the card feels bumpy around the birth date, and the person cannot recall their own ZIP code. What should you do?
correct: B. Refuse the sale, and hold the ID and notify a manager per house policy
wrong: A. Sell; the card is probably fine
C. Sell but only beer
D. Ask them to sign for it
feedback: Multiple warning signs -- mismatched photo, tampered feel, and failure to answer a basic verifying question -- mean you do not sell. Decline, and follow house policy on holding the ID and notifying a manager.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: An adult guest orders a beer, then immediately slides it to a visibly underage companion at the table. What is the correct response?
correct: B. Remove or refuse the drink -- transferring alcohol to someone under 21 is illegal, and you may not keep serving the pair for that purpose
wrong: A. Nothing; the adult bought it legally
C. Charge the underage guest for it
D. Ask them to move tables
feedback: Buying or transferring alcohol to a person under 21 for consumption is unlawful (a 'straw purchase'). Interrupt the transfer, refuse further service for that purpose, and involve a manager if needed.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer appears young but insists they are 21. You are unsure and the line is growing. What should you do?
correct: D. Request valid ID before selling
wrong: A. Complete the sale quickly
B. Ask another customer
C. Skip the ID check
feedback: Always verify age when unsure. Requesting valid ID is required to prevent underage sales and stay compliant with South Carolina law.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer cannot provide valid identification but insists they are over 21 and becomes frustrated. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse the sale without valid ID
wrong: A. Complete the sale to avoid conflict
B. Accept their verbal confirmation
D. Ask another customer to confirm
feedback: You must verify age with valid identification. If a customer cannot provide it, you are required to refuse the sale to remain compliant with South Carolina law.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer looks about 25 and says they forgot their ID. They insist they are old enough. What should you do?
correct: D. Refuse the sale without ID
wrong: A. Complete the sale based on appearance
B. Accept their verbal confirmation
C. Ask a coworker to decide
feedback: You must verify age with valid identification. If a customer cannot provide ID, you are required to refuse the sale to stay compliant with South Carolina

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer presents an ID that appears valid, but it is expired. They insist it should still be accepted. What should you do?
correct: D. Refuse the sale due to expired ID
wrong: A. Accept it because the birthdate is correct
B. Ignore the expiration date
C. Ask another customer
feedback: Expired IDs are not valid for alcohol sales. You must verify age using a current, valid form of identification to remain compliant with South Carolina law.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer presents an ID that looks real, but the print appears blurry and the edges seem uneven. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse the sale due to suspicious ID
wrong: A. Accept the ID and continue
B. Ignore the quality issues
D. Ask another customer
feedback: Signs of tampering or poor quality can indicate a fake ID. You should refuse the sale if you cannot confirm the ID is valid.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer correctly answers questions about their ID, but you still feel unsure about its authenticity. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse the sale if unsure
wrong: A. Complete the sale anyway
B. Trust the answers and proceed
D. Skip further checks
feedback: If you are not confident the ID is valid, you must refuse the sale. Uncertainty means you cannot verify legal eligibility.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer becomes frustrated when you take extra time to check their ID carefully. What is the best response?
correct: C. Continue carefully and complete all checks
wrong: A. Rush the process to avoid conflict
B. Skip steps to move faster
D. Ignore the ID
feedback: You must complete all ID-checking steps regardless of pressure. Taking your time ensures compliance and prevents illegal sales.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: Today’s date is June 1. A customer’s date of birth is June 5, 2005. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse the sale because they are not yet 21
wrong: A. Complete the sale because they turn 21 soon
B. Ignore the exact date
D. Ask another customer
feedback: The customer must be fully 21 on the day of the sale. If their birthday has not yet occurred, you must refuse the sale.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer presents an ID with a clear photo, but the birthdate looks scratched and altered. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse the sale due to suspected tampering
wrong: A. Accept the ID because the photo matches
B. Ignore the altered area
D. Ask another customer
feedback: Signs of alteration indicate the ID may be fake. You must refuse the sale if you cannot confirm the ID is valid.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer presents an ID that seems valid, but their answers do not match the information. They become impatient and ask you to hurry. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse the sale due to uncertainty
wrong: A. Complete the sale to avoid conflict
B. Ignore the inconsistencies
D. Ask another customer
feedback: If you cannot confirm the ID is valid, you must refuse the sale. Inconsistencies and pressure are warning signs that require you to stop the transaction.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer without ID becomes loud and insists you serve them because others are waiting. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse the sale and involve a manager
wrong: A. Serve them to avoid disruption
B. Argue until they leave
D. Ignore the situation
feedback: You must refuse the sale and involve a manager if needed. Staying calm and following procedures helps manage conflict safely while remaining compliant.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer gives an ID, but the photo does not clearly match their face. What should you do?
correct: D. Refuse the sale due to mismatch
wrong: A. Accept it if they seem confident
B. Ignore the photo mismatch
C. Ask another customer
feedback: The ID must match the person presenting it. If the photo does not match, you cannot verify identity and must refuse the sale.

section: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability
question: A customer cannot answer basic questions about their ID and avoids eye contact. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse the sale due to uncertainty
wrong: A. Complete the sale
B. Ignore the behavior
D. Let a coworker handle it
feedback: Inconsistent answers and behavior are warning signs. If you cannot confirm the ID is valid, you must refuse the sale.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest is slurring words, swaying on the stool, and just knocked over a glass while reaching for their drink. What should you do?
correct: B. Stop alcohol service, offer water or food, and involve a manager while arranging safe transportation
wrong: A. Serve one more since they are still sitting down
C. Wait until they fall before acting
D. Move them to a booth and keep serving
feedback: Slurred speech, swaying, and loss of coordination are clear signs of intoxication. Stop serving alcohol, offer water or food, involve a manager, and help arrange safe transportation.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest begins speaking loudly, stumbling slightly, and struggling to focus on conversation. What stage of impairment are they most likely in?
correct: C. Mid-stage impairment​
wrong: A. Early stage​
B. No impairment​
D. Severe intoxication
feedback: These signs show clear physical and mental impairment. Mid-stage intoxication includes slurred speech, poor coordination, and confusion. At this point, you should stop or significantly limit alcohol service to prevent further intoxication and remain compliant with the law.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest is stumbling, has slurred speech, and is spilling their drink repeatedly. What should you do?
correct: D. Stop alcohol service
wrong: A. Continue serving as long as they pay​
B. Ignore the behavior unless they complain​
C. Offer food and another drink​
feedback: Stumbling, slurred speech, and loss of coordination are clear physical signs of intoxication. Continuing to serve alcohol would be unsafe and illegal. You must stop service and take steps to manage the situation responsibly.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest becomes loud, interrupts others, and starts arguing with staff after several drinks. What is the best action?
correct: D. Stop alcohol service
wrong: A. Continue serving to calm them down​
B. Ignore the behavior unless it worsens​
C. Offer another drink to distract them​
feedback: Argumentative behavior, loud speech, and poor judgment are clear behavioral signs of intoxication. Continuing to serve alcohol would increase risk and may be illegal. You should stop service and take steps to manage the situation safely.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest has not eaten, is drinking quickly, and is in a loud group celebrating. What is the best approach?
correct: C. Slow service and monitor closely​
wrong: A. Continue normal service since they seem happy​
B. Ignore the environment and focus only on drink count​
D. Serve stronger drinks to match the group
feedback: Drinking quickly, an empty stomach, and a high-energy environment all increase intoxication risk. Slowing service and monitoring closely helps prevent over-service and keeps you compliant with South Carolina law.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest is ordering drinks quickly and has not eaten. What is the best way to prevent over-service?
correct: C. Slow service and suggest food or water​
wrong: A. Serve faster to keep up with demand​
B. Ignore it unless they appear intoxicated​
D. Offer stronger drinks to reduce orders
feedback: Slowing service and offering food or water helps reduce the rate of alcohol consumption. These techniques are effective ways to prevent over-service and keep guests safe while staying compliant with South Carolina law.*

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest is repeatedly ordering drinks, showing mood swings, and ignoring your attempts to slow service. What should you do?
correct: C. Stop alcohol service and involve a manager​
wrong: A. Continue serving to avoid conflict​
B. Ignore the behavior unless it escalates further​
D. Offer another drink to calm them down
feedback: Repeated ordering, mood swings, and ignoring limits are clear signs of problem drinking and increasing intoxication. You must stop alcohol service and involve management to handle the situation safely and remain compliant with South Carolina law.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest orders two drinks quickly and begins speaking loudly. They are laughing and encouraging others to drink faster. What is your best first step?
correct: B. Slow service and monitor​
wrong: A. Ignore the behavior​
C. Serve another round​
D. Ask them to leave immediately
feedback: Correct.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest has glassy eyes, is repeating themselves, and drops their credit card twice. What do these signs indicate?
correct: C. Mid-level impairment​
wrong: A. No concern​
B. Early intoxication​
D. Severe intoxication
feedback: Repetition, poor coordination, and visible physical signs indicate clear impairment. Service should be limited or stopped.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest has not eaten, is drinking quickly, and is part of a loud celebration group. What risk is present?
correct: D. High risk
wrong: A. Low risk​
B. No risk​
C. Moderate risk​
feedback: Fast drinking, group pressure, and an empty stomach all increase intoxication risk. This situation requires close monitoring and slower service.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest insists they are fine but is stumbling and knocking into furniture. What should you rely on?
correct: C. Your observations​
wrong: A. Guest’s statement​
B. Drink count only​
D. Other guests’ opinions
feedback: You must base decisions on observable behavior. Physical signs like stumbling show clear impairment regardless of what the guest says.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A guest becomes argumentative and ignores your attempts to slow service. What is the best next step?.
correct: C. Stop service and involve manager​
wrong: A. Continue serving​
B. Avoid confrontation​
D. Offer a stronger drink
feedback: Argumentative behavior and refusal to follow limits are signs of intoxication. You must stop service and involve management.

section: Unit 4 — Recognizing Intoxication
question: A quiet guest appears calm but has slow reactions and unfocused eyes. What should you do?
correct: C. Monitor and slow service​
wrong: A. Ignore them​
B. Serve normally​
D. Ask them to leave immediately
feedback: Not all intoxicated guests are loud. Subtle signs like slow reactions still require monitoring and controlled service.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A guest becomes irritated after you refuse another drink and says, 'I know the owner. Serve me anyway.' What should you do?
correct: B. Stay calm, repeat the refusal, offer water or a nonalcoholic option, and involve a manager if the guest continues to push
wrong: A. Serve the drink to avoid conflict
C. Argue until they admit they are intoxicated
D. Ignore them and walk away without telling anyone
feedback: A refusal should be calm, firm, and respectful. Repeat the boundary, offer alternatives, and involve a manager when the guest resists or escalates.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A server cuts off a visibly intoxicated guest. The guest leaves angry and starts walking toward the parking lot with car keys in hand. What is the best next step?
correct: B. Follow escalation procedures: alert a manager or security, try to arrange safe transportation, and document the incident
wrong: A. Do nothing because service already stopped
C. Serve coffee and assume they will sober up immediately
D. Let them drive if they promise to go slowly
feedback: Stopping service is only part of responsible service. Escalate, involve management/security, arrange safe transportation when possible, and document the incident according to house policy.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A customer is slurring words, stumbling, and becoming loud. They ask for another drink. What should you do?
correct: C. Refuse service and notify management
wrong: A. Serve one more drink and monitor
B. Ask a coworker to decide
D. Ignore the behavior
feedback: Refusing service is required when a customer shows clear signs of intoxication. Involving management helps ensure safety and proper handling of the situation.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: You observe a customer showing clear signs of intoxication. What is the correct first step?
correct: D. Observe carefully and confirm signs
wrong: A. Immediately call security
B. Ignore it unless they ask for a drink
C. Offer water and continue service
feedback: The first step is to observe and confirm signs of intoxication. Accurate assessment ensures that your refusal decision is appropriate and based on clear evidence.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: After refusing service, a customer continues asking for another drink. What should you do?
correct: D. Maintain refusal and involve management if needed
wrong: A. Serve one last drink to calm them
B. Ignore them and walk away
C. Change your decision if they insist
feedback: Once service is refused, the decision must remain firm. If the customer persists or escalates, involving management helps ensure the situation is handled safely and correctly.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A customer becomes upset after being refused service and raises their voice. What is the best response?
correct: D. Stay calm and repeat your refusal clearly
wrong: A. Raise your voice to be heard
B. Ignore them completely
C. Argue your decision in detail
feedback: Staying calm and repeating your refusal clearly helps prevent escalation. Matching the customer’s emotions or arguing can make the situation worse. Professional communication is key to maintaining control.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A customer becomes confrontational after being refused service and begins arguing loudly. What should you do?
correct: C. Stay calm and involve management if needed
wrong: A. Argue back to defend your decision
B. Ignore the situation completely
D. Serve them to avoid conflict
feedback: Staying calm and involving management helps control the situation safely. Arguing or giving in can escalate risk and lead to violations or unsafe conditions.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A customer becomes loud, refuses to leave, and starts making threats after being refused service. What should you do?
correct: D. Involve management immediately and maintain distance
wrong: A. Try to physically remove the customer yourself
B. Ignore the behavior and continue working
C. Argue with the customer to gain control
feedback: Involving management and maintaining a safe distance is the correct response. Physical involvement or arguing can increase risk and lead to unsafe outcomes.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A customer has been refused alcohol due to intoxication. What is the best next step?
correct: C. Offer water or help them find a safe ride
wrong: A. Serve a smaller drink instead
B. Ignore the customer completely
D. Ask another server to serve them
feedback: Offering water or helping arrange safe transportation supports safety after refusal. Continuing alcohol service or ignoring the situation increases risk and violates responsible service practices.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: After refusing service to an intoxicated customer, what should you do to protect yourself legally?
correct: D. Document the incident according to policy
wrong: A. Ignore the situation and move on
B. Only tell a coworker verbally
C. Change the story to avoid trouble
feedback: Proper documentation creates a clear record of your actions. This helps show that you followed the law and workplace procedures if the situation is reviewed later.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A customer shows slurred speech and poor balance but asks for another drink. What should you do?
correct: B. Refuse service and stay calm
wrong: A. Serve one more drink and monitor
C. Ask another server to handle it
D. Ignore the signs
feedback: Visible signs of intoxication require refusal of service. Staying calm and professional helps prevent escalation and ensures you follow South Carolina requirements.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A customer becomes upset after being refused and raises their voice. What is the best response?
correct: C. Stay calm and repeat refusal
wrong: A. Raise your voice back
B. Walk away immediately
D. Serve them to calm them down
feedback: Staying calm and repeating your refusal clearly helps de-escalate the situation. Matching their behavior or giving in increases risk and violates responsible service practices.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: After refusing service, a customer asks for “just water” but continues requesting alcohol. What should you do?
correct: B. Maintain refusal and offer water only
wrong: A. Serve alcohol with the water
C. Ignore them completely
D. Ask a coworker to serve them
feedback: You can offer safe alternatives like water, but alcohol service must remain stopped. Consistency is critical once refusal has been made.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: A customer refuses to leave after being denied service and begins bothering other guests. What is the correct action?
correct: C. Involve management or security
wrong: A. Handle it alone
B. Ignore the behavior
D. Offer another drink
feedback: Disruptive behavior requires escalation. Management or security should handle situations that affect safety or other customers.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: You refused service and the customer becomes quiet but unsteady. What should you do next?
correct: B. Monitor and ensure safety
wrong: A. Resume service if they calm down
C. Ignore them
D. Ask them to leave immediately without support
feedback: Continued monitoring is important after refusal. Even if behavior calms, impairment may still be present and requires attention.

section: Unit 5 — Intervention and Refusal Skills
question: After a difficult refusal, what step helps protect you legally?
correct: C. Document the incident
wrong: A. Do nothing
B. Only tell a coworker
D. Change your actions later
feedback: Documentation creates a clear record of your actions. It helps show that you followed proper procedures and supports you if the situation is reviewed later.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: A customer has had several drinks over a short period and is beginning to show signs of intoxication. What should you do next?
correct: C. Slow or stop service and monitor the situation
wrong: A. Continue serving as long as they are not causing problems
B. Ignore the behavior and let a coworker handle it
D. Serve water but continue alcohol service
feedback: Slowing or stopping service helps prevent over-intoxication and reduces risk. Acting early is part of responsible alcohol service.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: You hear that South Carolina may have changed a rule about serving hours, but you are not sure. What is the best professional response?
correct: B. Check current South Carolina resources or ask your manager, and complete refresher training to stay current
wrong: A. Keep serving the way you always have until someone complains
C. Guess based on what other bars do
D. Ignore it; training only matters when you are first hired
feedback: Laws and rules change over time. Staying current through refresher training and checking authoritative South Carolina resources or your manager is part of responsible, ongoing professional conduct.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: A customer becomes upset after you refuse service due to signs of intoxication. They raise their voice and demand another drink. What should you do next?
correct: D. Involve a manager and remain calm
wrong: A. Continue serving to avoid conflict
B. Ignore the customer and walk away
C. Argue to prove your decision is correct
feedback: Involving a manager helps handle conflict safely and supports your decision to refuse service. Team accountability ensures situations are managed properly.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: A group is participating in a drink special and begins ordering drinks quickly. One customer is showing early signs of intoxication. What should you do?
correct: C. Slow or stop service and monitor the customer
wrong: A. Continue serving because it is part of the promotion
B. Ignore the signs and focus on other customers
D. Serve water but continue alcohol service
feedback: Promotions do not change your responsibility. Slowing or stopping service helps prevent over-intoxication and keeps the situation safe.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: A customer orders a strong cocktail with multiple shots and finishes it quickly. They ask for another right away. What should you do?
correct: C. Recognize it may equal multiple drinks and slow service
wrong: A. Serve another immediately since it is one drink
B. Ignore the strength and continue normal service
D. Only count it as one drink and continue serving
feedback: A strong cocktail may contain more than one standard drink. Slowing service helps prevent over-intoxication and supports safe decision-making.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: A coworker continues serving a customer who appears intoxicated. What should you do?
correct: C. Address the concern or involve a manager
wrong: A. Ignore the situation and focus on your own tables
B. Support the coworker and continue service
D. Wait until the situation becomes worse
feedback: Team accountability means speaking up and involving management when needed. Addressing unsafe service early helps protect everyone and supports workplace standards.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: A customer shows an ID that looks damaged and hard to read. You are unsure if it is valid. What should you do?
correct: B. Refuse service or ask for another valid ID
wrong: A. Accept it if the customer insists it is real
C. Ignore the issue and continue service
D. Let a coworker decide
feedback: If you cannot verify an ID, do not serve alcohol. Always require clear and valid identification before serving.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: You are unsure whether a customer is intoxicated. What is the best next step?
correct: C. Involve a manager for support
wrong: A. Continue serving to be safe
B. Ask the customer directly
D. Ignore the concern
feedback: When unsure, seek help. Managers can provide guidance and help make the correct decision.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: A busy shift makes it hard to track how much customers are drinking. What should you do?
correct: C. Work with your team to monitor
wrong: A. Stop tracking and focus on speed
B. Guess based on appearance
D. Ignore consumption levels
feedback: Team communication helps track consumption and maintain safe service during busy periods.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: A customer asks for multiple drinks at once for themselves. What is your best response?
correct: C. Limit service and monitor behavior
wrong: A. Serve all drinks immediately
B. Refuse without explanation
D. Ignore the request
feedback: Limiting service helps prevent rapid consumption and reduces risk of intoxication.

section: Unit 6 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture
question: A coworker refuses service and the customer asks you for a drink instead. What should you do?
correct: C. Support the refusal and do not serve
wrong: A. Serve them to help the customer
B. Ignore your coworker’s decision
D. Let the customer decide
feedback: Team consistency is critical. Supporting refusals helps maintain safe and legal service.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: A guest says they can refuse a breath test because they never agreed to testing. What should a server understand about South Carolina law?
correct: B. Driving in South Carolina is treated as implied consent to testing, and refusal can trigger an automatic license suspension
wrong: A. Refusal has no consequence if the person is polite
C. Implied consent applies only to commercial drivers
D. Refusal is allowed after midnight
feedback: Under South Carolina implied-consent law, driving in the state is treated as consent to testing when an alcohol- or drug-related violation is alleged. Refusal can trigger an automatic license suspension.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: A guest wants to take an unfinished open beer in the car for the ride home. What is the correct response?
correct: B. Do not allow it; South Carolina prohibits open containers of beer, wine, or liquor in a moving vehicle except in the luggage compartment
wrong: A. Allow it if the passenger holds it
C. Allow it if the driver is not intoxicated
D. Pour it into a coffee cup
feedback: South Carolina prohibits open containers of beer, wine, or liquor in a moving vehicle, except in the luggage compartment. Do not let guests leave with open alcohol for the car.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: According to NHTSA FARS data, roughly what share of drivers in South Carolina's fatal crashes in 2023 had a blood alcohol concentration of 0.08% or higher?
correct: C. About 39%
wrong: A. About 10%
B. About 25%
D. About 75%
feedback: In 2023, about 39% of drivers in South Carolina fatal crashes had a BAC of 0.08% or higher, and 413 people were killed in alcohol-impaired-driving crashes (NHTSA FARS, 2023).

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: A guest has been drinking for several hours. It is late, and they plan to drive home. They show slower reactions and reduced awareness. What is the best action?
correct: D. Stop service and intervene
wrong: A. Serve another drink
B. Ignore the situation
C. Suggest they leave quickly
feedback: Stopping service and intervening helps prevent impaired driving and supports responsible alcohol service practices in South Carolina.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: A guest is stumbling, bumps into others, and drops a glass. They laugh and ask for another drink. What is the best action?
correct: D. Stop service and intervene
wrong: A. Serve another drink
B. Ignore the behavior
C. Ask them to be careful and continue
feedback: The guest shows clear signs of impairment and injury risk. Stopping service helps prevent harm and supports responsible alcohol service.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: A group becomes loud and disruptive near closing time. One guest is clearly intoxicated and tries to leave to drive home. Nearby customers are uncomfortable. What should you do?
correct: D. Stop service and address the situation
wrong: A. Ignore the group
B. Serve another round
C. Ask them to quiet down and continue service
feedback: Stopping service and intervening helps prevent harm to the community and supports public safety responsibilities tied to alcohol service.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: An officer enters your establishment during a busy shift and observes service. A guest who appears underage approaches the bar. What should you do?
correct: D. Check ID before serving
wrong: A. Serve quickly to keep the line moving
B. Skip ID since they look confident
C. Ask a coworker to handle it
feedback: Checking ID every time is required and helps ensure compliance during enforcement observation.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: A guest shows clear signs of intoxication but insists they are fine and asks for another drink. What should you do?
correct: D. Stop service and intervene
wrong: A. Serve one more to avoid conflict
B. Ignore the signs
C. Let another server decide
feedback: Real-world cases show that ignoring clear signs leads to harm. Stopping service helps prevent injury and supports responsible alcohol service.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: A guest has had several drinks and begins speaking slowly. They ask for another drink and mention driving home. What should you do?
correct: D. Stop service and intervene
wrong: A. Serve one more drink
B. Ignore the comment
C. Suggest they hurry home
feedback: Stopping service helps prevent impaired driving and aligns with responsible alcohol service practices.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: A coworker continues serving a guest who appears intoxicated. What is the best response?
correct: C. Communicate concern
wrong: A. Ignore it
B. Join in
D. Serve faster
feedback: Team communication helps prevent over-service and supports safe practices.

section: Unit 7 — SC DUI, Enforcement, and Real-World Consequences
question: A guest tries to leave quickly after heavy drinking and says they are driving. What should you do?
correct: C. Intervene and stop service
wrong: A. Let them go
B. Encourage speed
D. Ignore the situation
feedback: Intervening helps prevent impaired driving and protects public safety.
